content:
Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation Kaiqiang He a , Fanmengjing Wang a, Hongyu Ma a , Zhouyou Wang a, Fengyan Shi a, Qi Wu a, Zhengchen Li a, Durga Acharya b, Cara M. Doherty b, Zhikao Li a, Douglas R. MacFarlane c, Huanting Wang a,* a Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Monash University, Clayton, Victoria, 3800, Australiab Manufacturing, CSIRO, Clayton, Victoria, 3168, Australiac School of Chemistry, Monash University, Clayton, Victoria, 3800, Australia

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Nafion composite membraneSpace confinementIon-selective nanochannelLi+/Mg2+ separation

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: A B S T R A C T The rising demand for lithium in energy storage technologies has increased the need for efficient and sustainable extraction methods. Seawater and brines contain abundant lithium reserves, yet separating lithium from coex­isting magnesium remains challenging due to their similar ionic properties. Membrane-based separation offers an energy-efficient and environmentally friendly approach for lithium recovery. In this study, a tetrapropy­lammonium (TPA)-modified Nafion/polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanochannel membrane was developed to achieve highly efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation. The hydrophobic PVDF matrix restricts Nafion swelling, ensuring stable channel dimensions. TPA cations interact with Nafion’s sulfonate groups to promote uniform dispersion and facilitate the formation of continuous ion-conducting nanochannels. The optimized TPA-Nafion/PVDF membrane exhibits a lithium flux of 26 mmol m-2 h-1 and a Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of approximately 42, demonstrating outstanding performance compared to advanced membranes. Besides, it maintains stable per­formance during extended operation. This work provides a practical strategy for constructing high-performance polymer membranes with tunable ion transport channels from widely accessible materials, paving the way for energy-efficient and cost-effective lithium extraction.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: 1. Introduction The rapid growth in lithium consumption, driven by its essential role in rechargeable batteries, electric vehicles, and other energy storage systems, has aroused the urgent need for sustainable lithium extraction techniques [1,2]. While conventional mining remains the primary source, it demands substantial resources and places a heavy burden on the environment. Seawater and salt lake brines, which contain vast lithium reserves, offer promising alternatives [3]. Among the various recovery technologies, solar evaporation and chemical leaching are widely used but often limited by long processing time, high reagent consumption, and environmental concerns. In contrast, membrane-based separation stands out for its potential to operate continuously with lower energy consumption, reduced chemical use, and minimal environmental footprint [4]. Current membrane technologies, such as nanofiltration, ion exchange membranes, and functionalized porous membranes, have demonstrated the ability to separate lithium ions based on size exclu­sion, charge selectivity, or chemical affinity [5–12]. However, these approaches often face a critical trade-off between ion selectivity and permeability, especially when discriminating between lithium and chemically similar cations like magnesium (Mg2+), which coexist at much higher concentrations.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: ity, especially when discriminating between lithium and chemically similar cations like magnesium (Mg2+), which coexist at much higher concentrations. Additionally, advanced materials like MOFs [13–17] and COFs [18–22], and MXenes [23–25] require precise synthesis, complex functionalization, and intricate fabrication steps to produce defect-free and highly-selective membranes, which limits their reproducibility and scalability. Therefore, there is a clear need for robust designs that combine high Li+ selectivity, fast transport, and long-term durability, while being manufacturable through simple processes from widely accessible materials. Nafion, a commercial perfluorosulfonic acid ionomer widely used in fuel cells [26–28], exhibits significant potential as a material for

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: Available online 8 October 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). lithium-ion separation due to its unique structure and properties. Its hydrophobic polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) backbone and hydrophilic sulfonate-group-containing side chains enable the formation of inter­connected hydrophilic channels upon water absorption, allowing effi­cient cation transport [29]. The cation transport within Nafion occurs via two main pathways, including bulk transport, where hydrated cat­ions diffuse through water-occupied channels, and surface transport, involving cation hopping between sulfonate groups [30]. The high chemical affinity of sulfonate groups for specific cations presents an opportunity to selectively separate lithium ions from other coexisting cations in seawater [13,31,32]. Under dry conditions, Nafion exhibits low ion conductivity. Upon hydration, microphase separation generates ionic clusters and hydrophilic channels. However, excessive water ab­sorption, driven by strong electrostatic interaction between water mol­ecules and sulfonate groups, can lead to enlarged channels (1–5 nm) and reduced site density of sulfonate groups, thereby impairing selectivity [33,34].

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: ules and sulfonate groups, can lead to enlarged channels (1–5 nm) and reduced site density of sulfonate groups, thereby impairing selectivity [33,34]. Thus, effective lithium-ion separation with Nafion requires limiting overswelling to prevent the formation of large, non-selective hydrophilic channels, while fully utilizing its sulfonate groups for effi­cient ion discrimination. To construct Nafion nanochannels for efficient lithium extraction, our strategy involves two key design principles aimed at optimizing the membrane’s structure and performance (Fig. 1). Firstly, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), known for its excellent compatibility with Nafion, is introduced as a hydrophobic and dense matrix to restrict Nafion’s excessive swelling during water absorption. By limiting the expansion of hydrophilic channels, the PVDF matrix ensures controlled channel di­mensions, enhancing ion selectivity while maintaining sufficient ionic conductivity. In addition, tetrapropylammonium (TPA) cations are incorporated to uniformly disperse Nafion within PVDF by interacting with its sulfonate groups and preventing aggregate formation. This work presents an innovative strategy for designing polymer membranes with cation-selective channels derived from conventional ionomers, paving the way for high-performance membranes for energy-efficient lithium extraction. 2. Materials and methods 2.1.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: rived from conventional ionomers, paving the way for high-performance membranes for energy-efficient lithium extraction. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Materials Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, average Mw ~534,000 by GPC), Nafion 117 solution (5 % in a mixture of lower aliphatic alcohols and water), tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH, 1 M in H2O), NMethyl-2-piperidone (NMP, ≥99 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), lithium chloride (LiCl, ≥99 %), and magnesium chloride (MgCl2, ≥98 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents were used as received without further purification.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: 2.2. Membrane preparation The TPA-Nafion/PVDF composite membranes were prepared using a blade-casting method. PVDF was first dissolved in NMP under contin­uous stirring at 80 ◦C to form a 10 wt% solution. Separately, a specific amount of Nafion 117 solution was cast into a Petri dish and dried at 80 ◦C for 3 h, yielding a thin Nafion membrane. The solid membrane was then immersed in an excess of 0.1 M TPAOH solution and gently stirred at room temperature for 12 h to enable cation exchange. To ensure full exchange, the membrane was subsequently transferred into a fresh 0.1 M TPAOH solution and soaked for an additional 12 h. After ion ex­change, the membrane was thoroughly rinsed with DI water to remove residual TPAOH. Using the solid membrane instead of the dispersion facilitated more effective ion exchange, as the hydrated matrix allowed TPA+ to diffuse uniformly and access sulfonate groups that would otherwise remain buried in the colloidal dispersion. The TPA-Nafion membrane was then added to the PVDF/NMP solution at varying Nafion loadings (2.5, 5, 10, and 20 wt% relative to PVDF) and stirred at 80 ◦C for 3 h to achieve a homogeneous dispersion. The mixture was then sonicated for 30 min and vacuum degassed for 3 h to remove air bubbles. The final membrane was obtained by blade-casting the solution onto a glass plate, followed by drying at 150 ◦C for 3 h. The samples were stored in DI water to keep hydrated. 2.3.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: ned by blade-casting the solution onto a glass plate, followed by drying at 150 ◦C for 3 h. The samples were stored in DI water to keep hydrated. 2.3. Membrane characterization Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were captured using a Nova NanoSEM 450, with all samples coated with iridium. An acceler­ating voltage of 5 kV was used, and a high voltage of 10 kV was applied for energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping. For preparing crosssectional SEM samples, the membrane was first immersed in liquid ni­trogen for approximately 1 min, then quickly fractured in half after removal. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were ob­tained using a FEI Tecnai G2 T20 operated at 200 kV. Membranes were stained by immersion in a lead (II) acetate aqueous solution, followed by multiple rinses with DI water. After drying, the stained membranes were embedded in epoxy resin, sectioned into ultrathin slices using an ul­tramicrotome, and mounted onto copper grids for imaging. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data for the membrane samples were acquired using an XRD instrument (Rigaku Miniflex600) with a Cu Kα radiation source (40 kV, 15 mA) at a scan rate of 2◦/min. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the membranes were recorded using an FTIR spec­trometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two), with results averaged from 10 measurements.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: ared (FTIR) spectra of the membranes were recorded using an FTIR spec­trometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two), with results averaged from 10 measurements. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a simultaneous thermal analyzer (TA Instruments SDT 650) at a heating rate of 10 ◦C min-1 under a nitrogen atmosphere. The water contact angles of the samples were measured using a video-based contact angle goniometer (Dataphysics model OCA15). Each sample was tested at five

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: Fig. 1. Schematic showing the fabrication process of the TPA-Nafion/PVDF membrane and the selective ion transport through space-confined Nafion nanochannels. K. He et al. Journal of Membrane Science 738 (2026) 124817 different surface locations, and the average value was calculated. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted to characterize the surface morphology and phase separation of the membrane samples using a Bruker Dimension Icon in tapping mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using a Thermo Scientific Nexsa Surface Analysis System equipped with a hemispherical analyzer. Monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) was used as the excitation source. Survey spectra were collected at a pass energy of 150 eV with a step size of 1.0 eV, while high-resolution spectra were acquired at a pass energy of 50 eV with a step size of 0.1 eV. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) measurements were carried out using a Bruker NanoSTAR II instrument, with the scattering vector (q) range set from 0.01 to 4 nm-1. Positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) was performed using Ortec EG&G spectrometers to determine the membrane pore size. Membrane samples were cut into 1 cm * 1 cm pieces and stacked into 3 mm-thick bundles for the measurements.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: eters to determine the membrane pore size. Membrane samples were cut into 1 cm * 1 cm pieces and stacked into 3 mm-thick bundles for the measurements. The molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of the membranes was determined using a solution-diffusion method with polyethylene glycol (PEG) molecules of varying molecu­lar weights (200, 400, 600, 1000, and 1500 Da), following established procedures in the literature [35]. The MWCO was defined as the mo­lecular weight corresponding to 90 % solute rejection, from which the effective pore cutoff size was estimated. 2.4. Li+/Mg2+ separation tests The Li+/Mg2+separation test was performed using a twocompartment Teflon cell setup following the procedure used in our group’s previous work (Fig. S1) [36]. A 0.1 M LiCl/MgCl2 mixed solu­tion was prepared by dissolving the respective salts in DI water. Before testing, the membranes were immersed in a 1:1 (v/v) ethanol-water solution for 3 h to ensure proper wetting. The membrane was clamped between the two Teflon cells, with the feed compartment filled with the LiCl/MgCl2 solution and the permeate compartment filled with an equal volume of DI water. A typical effective area of the membrane was around 50 mm2. A voltage of 1 V was applied across the membrane using a pair of Ag/AgCl electrodes, with the feed side connected to the positive electrode and the permeate side connected to the negative electrode of a DC power supply (ITECH IT6322).

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: with the feed side connected to the positive electrode and the permeate side connected to the negative electrode of a DC power supply (ITECH IT6322). During the test, small aliquots of the permeate solution were collected to determine the concentrations of Li+ and Mg2+ using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Thermo Scientific iCAP). The ion flux Ji (mmol⋅m-2⋅h-1) was calculated as Ji = (Ci -C0)Vt⋅A(1) where t stands for the testing time (h), A refers to the effective area of the membrane (m2), Ci and C0 refer to ion concentrations of the permeate solutions before and after t-hour test. The Li+/Mg2+ selectivity α was obtained from α = JLi+CMg2+ JMg2+CLi+(2) where JLi+ and JMg2+ stand for the ion fluxes of Li+ and Mg2+, and CLi+and CMg2+ refer to the initial feed concentrations of Li+ and Mg2+ in the feed solution, respectively. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Membrane fabrication and characterization The TPA-Nafion/PVDF membrane was fabricated via blade casting, following the homogeneous mixing of TPA+-exchanged Nafion with PVDF. The incorporation of TPA+ serves to regulate the hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups on the Nafion side chains, effectively suppressing the aggregation of Nafion domains within the hydrophobic PVDF matrix. As shown in the SEM surface and cross-sectional images (Fig. 2a and b), the resulting membrane displays a dense and uniform morphology with an average thickness of approximately 7.4 μm. SEM images in Fig.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: (Fig. 2a and b), the resulting membrane displays a dense and uniform morphology with an average thickness of approximately 7.4 μm. SEM images in Fig. S2 further reveal that all blade-cast polymer membranes, including PVDF, Nafion, and Nafion/PVDF composite membranes, exhibit dense cross-sectional and surface structures with uniform thicknesses of around 7 μm. Despite differences in composition, no apparent porosity or domain aggregation is observed, suggesting good compatibility between Nafion and PVDF, likely due to the chemical similarity between the PTFE backbone of Nafion and the PVDF matrix. Additionally, elemental mapping by EDX (Fig. 2c–f) confirms the ho­mogeneous distribution of TPA-Nafion within the PVDF matrix, further indicating successful integration and favorable interfacial compatibility between the two components. The TEM image in Fig. 2g shows dark regions corresponding to hy­drophilic domains containing ionic clusters within the composite membrane [37]. The average domain size (~3.7 nm) is significantly lower than that of the Nafion membrane (~7.3 nm, Fig. S3). This in­dicates that the PVDF matrix can effectively confine the space, leading to the formation of narrower Nafion nanochannels. The TGA data in Fig. 2h reveal the thermal stability of pure Nafion, PVDF, and the TPA-Nafion/PVDF composite membranes.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: narrower Nafion nanochannels. The TGA data in Fig. 2h reveal the thermal stability of pure Nafion, PVDF, and the TPA-Nafion/PVDF composite membranes. Nafion undergoes a three-stage degradation, including moisture loss (50–200 ◦C), decom­position of the sulfonic acid group (300–400 ◦C), and oxidative break­down of the polymer backbone (420–530 ◦C). PVDF shows a sharp degradation onset at around 430 ◦C, indicating its high thermal resis­tance. The composite membranes display two main degradation steps that incorporate characteristics from both components, with degrada­tion primarily starting from 340 ◦C. Compared to pure Nafion, the composite membranes show improved thermal stability and higher re­sidual mass, indicating enhanced robustness. The XRD patterns reveal distinct structural differences between the Nafion/PVDF composite membranes and their components (Fig. 2i). PVDF displays sharp diffraction peaks at approximately 18.5, 20, and 22.5◦, corresponding to its semi-crystalline α-phase and confirming the presence of well-defined crystalline domains [38]. In contrast, Nafion exhibits broad diffraction features centered around 17.5 and 40◦, sug­gesting its amorphous nature with short-range ordering attributed to ionic clusters [39]. As the Nafion content in the Nafion/PVDF composite membranes increases from 2.5 to 20 wt%, a progressive attenuation of PVDF’s crystalline peaks is observed.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: Nafion content in the Nafion/PVDF composite membranes increases from 2.5 to 20 wt%, a progressive attenuation of PVDF’s crystalline peaks is observed. Meanwhile, a broad diffraction feature near 17◦emerges and intensifies, consistent with the amorphous characteristics of Nafion. This interpretation is further supported by DSC analysis (Fig. S4), where neat PVDF exhibits a sharp melting endotherm at ~175 ◦C, while the Nafion/PVDF composite displays a broadened peak with reduced enthalpy, indicating suppressed crystallinity [40]. Together, the XRD and DSC results imply that Nafion is uniformly dispersed within the PVDF matrix and induces a composition-dependent reorganization of the membrane microstructure. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 2j also provide clear evidence for the suc­cessful incorporation of Nafion into the PVDF matrix. Characteristic absorption peaks, including the CF2 stretching vibration around 1180 cm-1 and the C–F bending vibration near 875 cm-1, remain prominent across all samples, indicating good chemical compatibility between PVDF and Nafion. This is attributed to the structural similarity between PVDF’s –(CH2–CF2)– and Nafion’s PTFE –(CF2–CF2)– backbones, which promotes molecular-level miscibility and suppresses phase separation during membrane formation.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: 2–CF2)– and Nafion’s PTFE –(CF2–CF2)– backbones, which promotes molecular-level miscibility and suppresses phase separation during membrane formation. Meanwhile, characteristic peaks associ­ated with Nafion, such as the symmetric S––O stretching vibration near 1230 cm-1 and the C–O–C stretching vibration of the ether side chain around 980 cm-1, become increasingly prominent with higher Nafion loadings [41]. The enhanced intensity of sulfonic acid-specific signals indicates an increase in functional group density and membrane hy­drophilicity, which is expected to facilitate cation transport across the membranes.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: 3.2. Regulated membrane swelling and phase separation Water contact angle measurements reveal a progressive increase in membrane surface hydrophilicity with higher Nafion content (Fig. 3a). The PVDF membrane exhibits a high contact angle of approximately 90◦, reflecting its hydrophobic nature. However, the Nafion membrane displays the lowest contact angle of around 62◦, confirming its highly hydrophilic character. As the Nafion content increases from 2.5 to 20 wt % in the composite membranes, the contact angle gradually decreases from 85 to 72◦, indicating the increasing incorporation of hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups into the PVDF matrix. This reduction in contact angle suggests improved surface wettability, which can facilitate water adsorption and promote the formation of more continuous hydrophilic pathways for ion transport. These results demonstrate that Nafion incorporation effectively tunes the membrane’s surface hydrophilicity, which is crucial for enhancing membrane hydration and ion conduction performance. Water uptake and swelling ratio measurements further highlight the enhanced hydrophilicity of PVDF membranes via Nafion integration (Fig. 3b). The PVDF membrane shows low water uptake (2.17 %) and a minimal swelling ratio (0.51 %), consistent with its dense structure and low polarity in an aqueous environment. However, the Nafion mem­brane exhibits the highest water uptake (15.98 %) and swelling ratio (7.65 %) due to its fully sulfonated structure.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: ironment. However, the Nafion mem­brane exhibits the highest water uptake (15.98 %) and swelling ratio (7.65 %) due to its fully sulfonated structure. Only a small addition of Nafion (2.5 wt%) leads to a noticeable increase in both water uptake (2.44 %) and swelling ratio (1.01 %), indicating the formation of wateraccessible domains. With increasing Nafion content to 5, 10, and 20 wt %, the water uptake rises to 5.26, 7.41, and 8.33 %, respectively, accompanied by corresponding increases in swelling ratio to 1.11, 1.55, and 2.06 %. Importantly, because these values are reported per total

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

(b) Cross-sectional SEM image of the membrane with corresponding EDX elemental mapping of (c) carbon, (d) fluorine, (e) sulfur, and (f) nitrogen. (g) TEM image of the membrane. (h) TGA curves, (i) XRD patterns, and (j) FTIR spectra of PVDF, Nafion, and Nafion/PVDF membranes with varying Nafion loadings (2.5, 5, 10, and 20 wt%).

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: Fig. 3. (a) Water contact angle, (b) water uptake and swelling ratio, and (c) SAXS profiles of PVDF, Nafion, and Nafion/PVDF composite membranes with different Nafion loadings (2.5, 5, 10, and 20 wt%). (d–i) AFM phase images of the PVDF, 2.5 wt% Nafion/PVDF, 5 wt% Nafion/PVDF, 10 wt% Nafion/PVDF, 20 wt% Nafion/ PVDF, and Nafion membranes. K. He et al. Journal of Membrane Science 738 (2026) 124817 membrane mass (PVDF + Nafion), the composites remain far below neat Nafion and reflect both dilution by PVDF and mechanical confinement of Nafion domains. Thus, while Nafion increases overall hydration, the PVDF matrix suppresses dimensional overswelling and preserves mem­brane integrity. This controlled swelling behavior is critical for ion separation applications, as it could restrict non-selective ion transport through over-swelled hydrophilic channels. The SAXS patterns in Fig. 3c provide insight into the nanoscale structural evolution of the membranes. For Nafion membrane, a broad scattering peak around 0.44 nm-1 indicates the presence of microphaseseparated ionic clusters [42]. In contrast, the Nafion/PVDF membranes exhibit weakened and right-shifted peaks, indicating disrupted long-range ionic ordering and a reduction in the average domain spacing. This shift to higher q-values corresponds to the formation of smaller and more uniformly distributed phase-separated domains.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: average domain spacing. This shift to higher q-values corresponds to the formation of smaller and more uniformly distributed phase-separated domains. The observed changes suggest that the incorporation of TPA cations and PVDF effectively suppresses the aggregation of Nafion-rich regions, leading to a more homogeneous nanoscale morphology. This structural reorganization contributes to improved dimensional stability and better swelling control upon hydration (Fig. 3b). These findings demonstrate that TPA cation exchange and PVDF blending not only refine the nanophase morphology but also enable more controlled and stable membrane hydration behavior. The surface morphology and phase separation behavior of the membranes were further characterized using AFM. As shown in the AFM height images (Figs. S5–6), Nafion membrane exhibits the lowest surface roughness owing to its uniform distribution of hydrophilic domains. However, PVDF membrane displays the highest surface roughness, which is attributed to the formation of crystalline domains such as spherulites that introduce topographical variations at the nanoscale. With increasing Nafion content (Fig. S5b–e), the surface roughness gradually decreases, resulting in a smoother morphology at higher loadings. This trend likely arises from Nafion’s disruption of PVDF crystallization, which suppresses crystalline domain formation and promotes more uniform polymer packing.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: kely arises from Nafion’s disruption of PVDF crystallization, which suppresses crystalline domain formation and promotes more uniform polymer packing. These findings suggest that Nafion incorporation modulates phase morphology and enhances interfacial uniformity, which could benefit ion transport performance. The AFM phase images, which imply variations in mechanical and adhesive properties, offer insight into the distribution of hydrophilic and hydrophobic domains (Fig. 3d–i). In these images, blue regions corre­spond to softer, more adhesive hydrophilic domains, typically associated with sulfonic acid clusters that absorb water and dissipate more energy, resulting in lower phase angles and darker contrast [43]. In contrast, red regions represent stiffer, less adhesive hydrophobic domains with higher phase angles. The Nafion/PVDF composite membranes exhibit a more apparent red and blue contrast, indicating enhanced phase separation between hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions and suggesting a more refined and stabilized nanophase-separated structure. This morphology implies that TPA cation modification, along with the spatial confine­ment effect of the PVDF matrix, promotes controlled domain distribu­tion and mitigates excessive swelling of hydrophilic regions upon hydration. Besides, an increasingly distinct nanoscale phase separation was observed with higher Nafion loadings, evidenced by greater contrast and a more well-defined domain distribution.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: stinct nanoscale phase separation was observed with higher Nafion loadings, evidenced by greater contrast and a more well-defined domain distribution. These changes in phase contrast reflect differences in the mechanical or viscoelastic properties between PVDFand Nafion-rich regions, highlighting the development of a more heterogeneous microstructure. 3.3. Effects of fabrication and composition on separation performance The Li+/Mg2+ separation performance of the TPA-Nafion/PVDF membranes was investigated using voltage-driven diffusion tests. The drying temperature during membrane fabrication after blade casting significantly influences the separation behavior of the 10 wt% TPANafion/PVDF membranes (Fig. 4a). As the drying temperature in­creases from 100 to 170 ◦C, the Li+ flux drops markedly, from approx­imately 469 mmol m-2 h-1 at 100 ◦C to 26 mmol m-2 h-1 at 150 ◦C, and further down to 3.3 mmol m-2 h-1 at 170 ◦C. This decline suggests that higher drying temperatures lead to excessive membrane densification and possible collapse of ion-conducting channels, which hinders ion transport. In contrast, the Li+/Mg2+ selectivity exhibits a volcanoshaped profile, where it rises from 4.4 at 100 ◦C to a maximum of 42 at 150 ◦C, before decreasing to 20 at 170 ◦C. This peak selectivity at 150 ◦C indicates an optimal nanoscale morphology, where favorable microphase separation and refined ion-conducting pathways enable selective Li+ transport.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: Fig. 4. (a) Li+/Mg2+ separation performance of 10 wt% Nafion/PVDF membranes fabricated at different drying temperatures. (b) Li+/Mg2+ separation performance of the Nafion/PVDF membrane with varying Nafion loadings. (c) Average pore diameter and (d) pore size distribution of PVDF, Nafion, and Nafion/PVDF composite membranes with different Nafion loadings (2.5, 5, 10, and 20 wt%). (e) Schematic illustration of Li+ and Mg2+ transport through the hydrophilic channels in Nafion and Nafion/PVDF membranes. (f) Schematic of the interactions between sulfonate groups and Li+/Mg2+ ions. K. He et al. Journal of Membrane Science 738 (2026) 124817

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: The Li+ flux increases steadily with Nafion loading, from approximately 3.4 mmol m-2 h-1 at 0 wt% (pure PVDF) to 1332 mmol m-2 h-1 at 100 wt% (pure Nafion), indicating that the increased ionomer fraction en­hances ion transport due to the formation of more hydrophilic pathways. However, the Li+/Mg2+ selectivity first rises from 1.8 at 0 wt% to a maximum of 42 at 10 wt%, and then sharply declines to below 5.4 at 20 wt% and 2.1 at 100 wt%. These results imply that low Nafion content enables selective ion conduction by forming well-confined ionic do­mains stabilized by the PVDF matrix, while excessive Nafion compro­mises ion selectivity due to overexpanded hydrophilic channels and diminished nanophase separation, as confirmed by swelling ratio, AFM, and SAXS analyses. Therefore, 10 wt% Nafion represents the optimal composition, offering a balance between efficient Li+ transport and high Li+/Mg2+ selectivity. To elucidate TPA+‘s role, we prepared a TPA+-free Nafion/PVDF control. This composite exhibited non-uniform ionomer dispersion with ~500 nm agglomerates (Fig. S7a). EDX point analyses indicate that the white domains in the SEM image are Nafion-rich with elevated oxygen and sulfur relative to the surrounding PVDF matrix (Fig. S7b). Corre­spondingly, the transport performance of the TPA+-free membranes was significantly poorer, with Li+ flux ~8.7 mmol m-2 h-1 and Li+/Mg2+ selectivity ~2.6 (Fig. S7c). Besides, XPS reveals the interaction between TPA+ and –SO3- (Fig. S8a).

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: rer, with Li+ flux ~8.7 mmol m-2 h-1 and Li+/Mg2+ selectivity ~2.6 (Fig. S7c). Besides, XPS reveals the interaction between TPA+ and –SO3- (Fig. S8a). A quaternary-ammonium N 1s peak at around 400 eV is observed only in TPA+-modified membranes (Fig. S8b) [44]. The S 2p peak remains in the sulfonate S(VI) range and shows a small positive shift (from 168.8 to 169.0 eV), consistent with ion pairing between TPA+ and –SO3- (Fig. S8c) [45]. Collectively, these observa­tions indicate that TPA+ electrostatically screens –SO3groups, reducing inter-ionomer attraction and limiting domain coalescence to yield a finer, more continuous ionomer morphology. The resulting well-connected nanochannels enable more efficient Li+ transport and improved ion discrimination. 3.4. Separation mechanisms of the membranes Pore size plays a critical role in understanding the separation mechanisms of the membranes. PALS was applied to measure the pore size distribution of the membranes, as shown in Fig. 4c. The average pore diameter of PVDF and low-Nafion-loaded composite membranes (2.5–20 wt%) remains in the narrow range of ~0.60–0.63 nm, indi­cating that small Nafion additions do not significantly alter the pore size of the PVDF matrix. However, a sharp increase to ~0.75 nm is observed for pure Nafion, consistent with its highly swollen sulfonated domains. The pore size distribution profiles in Fig.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: p increase to ~0.75 nm is observed for pure Nafion, consistent with its highly swollen sulfonated domains. The pore size distribution profiles in Fig. 4d reveal that PVDF and composite membranes with ≤20 wt% Nafion exhibit narrow peaks centered around ~0.6 nm, suggesting well-confined nanochannels. In contrast, pure Nafion shows a broader distribution and an apparent shift toward larger pore sizes, reflecting more open and interconnected hy­drophilic domains. Hydration has only a minor influence on the pore structure of the Nafion/PVDF membrane. PALS results show that the mean free-volume diameter increases only slightly from ~0.62 nm in the dry state to ~0.63 nm when hydrated (Fig. S9a), suggesting negligible sub-nanometer swelling. This observation is consistent with the MWCO measurement (~0.68 nm) (Fig. S9b), which confirms that the membrane maintains a narrow pore-size distribution and structural stability upon hydration. These results indicate that incorporating PVDF effectively suppresses Nafion domain swelling, maintaining smaller and more uniform ion-conducting channels in the composite membranes. The ion transport mechanisms and selective behavior in both Nafion and Nafion/PVDF composite membranes are elucidated in Fig. 4e. In the Nafion membrane, high water uptake leads to significant swelling of the sulfonated domains, forming enlarged and interconnected hydrophilic channels.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: Nafion membrane, high water uptake leads to significant swelling of the sulfonated domains, forming enlarged and interconnected hydrophilic channels. These broad channels facilitate the transport of both Li+ and Mg2+ ions with limited selectivity, since the ionic pathways are insuf­ficiently constrained to discriminate between monovalent and divalent cations. In contrast, the Nafion/PVDF composite membrane features narrower and more regulated hydrophilic channels. This refined morphology arises from the combination of PVDF’s hydrophobic confinement and the structural rearrangement induced by TPA cations during membrane formation. Although the hydrated diameters of Mg2+ (8.56 Å) and Li+ (7.64 Å) are larger than the membranes’ average pore size [46], transport proceeds via partial dehydration within the nano­channels. The higher dehydration penalty of Mg2+ imposes stronger steric hindrance and lowers its mobility, whereas Li+ can migrate effi­ciently through the confined channels [47]. This physical confinement is critical for enhancing Li+/Mg2+ selectivity while maintaining high Li + conductivity. The selective ion transport is governed not only by physical confinement but also by specific interactions between ions and func­tional groups within the membrane [48]. Fig. 4f further highlights the significant role of ion-specific interactions between sulfonic acid groups and the cations.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: s within the membrane [48]. Fig. 4f further highlights the significant role of ion-specific interactions between sulfonic acid groups and the cations. Li+ ions, due to their small size and lower charge density, exhibit weak electrostatic interactions with sulfonate groups with a binding energy of -0.77 eV [31] and can migrate rapidly via a hopping mechanism between adjacent sites. In contrast, Mg2+ ions possess a higher charge density and a strongly bound hydration shell, resulting in stronger coordination with sulfonic acid groups with a binding energy of -2.32 eV and a greater tendency to become immo­bilized within the nanochannels. This difference in binding affinity re­inforces the selective exclusion of Mg2+ from transmembrane transport pathways. The combination of structural modulation of ion channel dimensions and distinct electrostatic interactions between ions and functional groups synergistically contributes to the superior Li+/Mg2+ separation performance achieved by the Nafion/PVDF membranes. 3.5. Membrane durability and performance comparison The long-term ion separation performance of the 10 wt% TPANafion/PVDF membrane was evaluated over a continuous 72-h test (Fig. 5a). Throughout this period, the membrane maintained a consis­tently high Li+ flux of approximately 40 mmol m-2 h-1, while the Mg2+ flux remained significantly lower, around 1.5 mmol m-2 h-1. The Li+/ Mg2+ selectivity starts at ~43 at 12 h and gradually declines to ~30 at 72 h.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: g2+ flux remained significantly lower, around 1.5 mmol m-2 h-1. The Li+/ Mg2+ selectivity starts at ~43 at 12 h and gradually declines to ~30 at 72 h. Despite this slight decrease, the membrane demonstrated good stability, retaining relatively high selectivity and Li+ flux over long-term operation. EDX mapping of S and N shows uniform TPA-Nafion distri­bution with no depletion band after testing (Fig. S10a–c). XPS further indicates retention of the TPA-sulfonate association. The N 1s (quater­nary ammonium, ~400 eV) and S 2p (sulfonate, ~169 eV) features are unchanged before and after the long-term test, while the N/S atomic ratio shifts only slightly from 1.20 to 1.10 (Fig. S10d–g). These results support that most TPA+ remains associated with –SO3- during operation and that the PVDF matrix maintains channel integrity, so even if limited counter-ion exchange occurs, the Nafion-derived hydrophilic pathways remain structurally stable, sustaining high Li+ flux and selectivity over time. To further assess durability, the membrane was subjected to five consecutive 24 h cycles under identical conditions. The Li+ flux and Li+/ Mg2+ selectivity showed only slight fluctuations across the series (Fig. S11a), confirming robust operational stability. In addition, when tested in a synthetic Salar de Atacama brine, the membrane maintained a Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of ~40 over 72 h (Fig.

citation.title: Space-confined nafion nanochannels in PVDF membranes enable efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: ional stability. In addition, when tested in a synthetic Salar de Atacama brine, the membrane maintained a Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of ~40 over 72 h (Fig. S11b), indicating its chemical stability in a high-salinity, multicomponent environment relevant to practical lithium extraction. Furthermore, the excellent compatibility between PVDF and Nafion enables the formation of ho­mogeneous mixtures suitable for scale-up. Through doctor-blade cast­ing, thin films of approximately 15 * 9 cm were produced in the laboratory (Fig. S12), demonstrating the scalability of the membrane for real-world deployment. The Li+/Mg2+ separation performance of the TPA-Nafion/PVDF membranes was compared with various state-of-the-art membranes and commercial membranes reported in the literature, which were
